28. Variational Inference

Variational methods lack performance guarantees unlike stochastic approximation and asymptotic approximation.

Variational methods generally turn the task of evaluating a quantity into an optimization problem. Many fundamental variaitonal methods arise out of considering the problem of evaluating a partition function (normalizing cnostant in the distribution).

For example:

  • In Section 13.7* we described a method for evaluating KL divergence.

  • In VAE, the likelihood as a function of the decoder parameter s is untractable, so instead of optimizing that, you optimize the ELBO which depends on both and (the encoder)

    (28.1)

Remarks:

  • An analogous relation holds for continuous variables with differential entropy and integrals.
  • In statistical physics, the quantity in the context of Gibbs-Boltzmann distributions is referred to as the variational free energy of the system.
  • When the data is , a widely-used choice for the optimization domain of is distributions where are independent. This is called the mean-field approximation to .

28.2. Parameter Estimation in Exponential Families

Suppose we want to approximate an exponential family.

One way to perform the optimization is find the closest distribution in terms of KL divergence with , but computing the KL divergence requires knowing the partition function:

(28.6)

Since this is the log-loss penalty for inference as developed in like Section 15.

(We use divergence with in the first slot because it’s the ground truth31)

So we approximate instead using variational inference approach:

So the variational approach minimizes the I-projection instead of the M-projection above.

Okay so for each , we only have an approximation so the distribution via choosing . This is not useful — but the resulting distribution is a useful approximation and called the variational approximation of .

28.3. Latent Variable Models

Variational setup:

The marginal is the partition function of the latent variable predictor:

Hence we can use variational methods instead of directly computing it.

Define the following lower bound on the marginal likelihood:

The choice of that makes the bound as tight as possible is the I-projection of onto . The resulting value of the lower bound with the optimal is called evidence-based lower bound (ELBO)

The ELBO may be tractable if is chosen carefully.

Using it for estimation:

Remark:

28.4. Model Architecture Selection

Recall from the last seciton that we can write this as a -ary hypothes testing problem. The posterior is infeasible

since we need to sum over all the distributions in each hypothesis class for each class.

When variational approximations are applied to such problems, the resulting methods are referred to as variational Bayes. (close correspondence with latent variable problem in previous section), giving an approximation to . You compute expectation under the simpler distribution which might be more tractable like a Gaussian.
